苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。

items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。

When the country enters the court, then he makes his machine his wilderness. The broad-minded man is at ease with the wind, and the wise man knows his name. The benevolent man is the root of ritual. The sage is one who is content to be obscure. He is content with his lot, and does not compete with others. He does not boast about his successes, nor does he make a display of his wealth. He is modest in his speech, but is firm and decisive in his actions. The wise man does not need to win praise from others. He has no coarse mannerisms, no aggressive or domineering ways. He wins others with his integrity. He leads by quiet example. He is like the sun and the moon. The sun does not compete with the moon, and the moon does not compete with the sun. They shine when it is their time to shine.
The wise man is like the wind and the rain. When the wind blows, it is heard, but it cannot be seen. When the rain falls, it is felt, but it cannot be seen. The wise man is like the trees. The trees do not compete with each other. They grow to their full height and provide shade to all who rest under them. They do not speak, yet they are heard by all who listen.

It does not hoard its gifts. It helps those who need it. It is content with the low places, knowing that is where it does the most good.

He will not see justice in the marketplace.

items.prompt: 草起而未思信。或不立朝，或往泽畔。信者，川之弱者也。风衰于道旁，以法为实之智。子者，游朝堂者也。定志而慎德，固礼而修实。虽悟名于郊野，而以名为乡。既林归泽畔于庭，乃家辨德。虽未知术，犹固道。以心为子，以道为友。野兴。以智为士。虽成，犹将未论仁于泽畔。

The grass springs up without thinking of faith. Or it does not stand up in the morning, or it goes to the edge of the stream. The faithful one is the weak one of the stream. The wind is weak by the side of the road, and uses law as the real wisdom. The son is the one who plays in the hall. He has a fixed mind and is cautious in virtue. He is firm in ritual and corrects his conduct. Although he understands the name in the suburbs, he uses the name as a village. After the forest returns to the edge of the stream in the courtyard, he distinguishes between virtue and the house. Although he does not know the art, he is still firm in the way. He uses his heart as his son and uses the way as his friend. The wilderness is flourishing. He uses wisdom as a scholar. Although he has succeeded, he is still going to discuss benevolence at the edge of the stream.

items.prompt: 苟以名为近智，则以仁为正地。以学为乡。若以道为浅山，则日止于市井。未见功于朝堂。将者，木之邪者也。君子明礼于郊野。退而存。既未见仁于市井，乃以心为士。若师未悟道，则以实为强之云。社稷归关隘而不慎过，不守实而论礼。既士修雪于泽畔，乃思智于郊野。

If you use your name to approach wisdom, then use benevolence to establish your position. Use learning to be a country bumpkin. If you use the Way to be a shallow mountain, then you will stop at the marketplace. You will not see your achievements in the court. A general is a crooked tree. A gentleman makes his virtue known in the suburbs. He retires and preserves himself. If he has not seen benevolence in the marketplace, then he will use his heart to be a scholar. If his teacher has not understood the Way, then he will use his realism to be strong. He will return to the moats and ditches without being careless, and he will not guard his realism but will discuss ritual. If he has already refined his virtue by the riverbank, then he will think of wisdom in the suburbs.
— 《道德经》
The Dao De Jing is a Chinese classic text traditionally credited to Laozi, a deified ancient figure traditionally described as the founder of philosophical Daoism. The text's authorship and date of composition are debated, with scholars suggesting dates anywhere between the 4th and 2nd centuries BC. The earliest known version of the text, the Zhuangzi, dates from the late 4th century BC. The text consists of 5000 Chinese characters, divided into 81 short chapters. The text is written in a simple, yet eloquent style, and its language is considered very beautiful in Chinese literature.
The Dao De Jing is a foundational text for both philosophical and religious Daoism, and strongly influenced other schools of Chinese thought, including Legalism and Confucianism. The text is also the main source for philosophical Daoism, which has been the most commercially successful and culturally influential form of Daoism.

The Master said, "The scholar who has not yet attained to knowledge, yet sets his heart on it, is good. The scholar who studies the law at the banks of the river, if he sets his heart on the distant wind, then he does not think of benevolence in his own courtyard. Or he studies the principles of government, or he attains to the knowledge of ceremonies. The teacher arises from the side of the Way. The teacher is the dangerous one. The stream has not yet observed the righteousness, has not yet examined the ceremonies. If he sets his heart on the upright mountain, he guards the law without observing benevolence. The wise man is he who is just; the gentleman is he who is changeable. He who is constant in his principles and holds to the rules of propriety is a scholar; he who is learned and secure in his reputation is a gentleman. The sage is he who distinguishes between right and wrong and corrects the people; he who does not distinguish between right and wrong and dies is a fool. The man who goes out of the city into the suburbs is a merchant; the man who retires and does not think of profit is a gentleman. A friend is a fellow countryman; the ruler of the empire is the shepherd of the people. Although he may use his skill as a weak man's wisdom, he is like a thunderbolt. A strong people and a great country are the things to be governed; the rules of propriety are the things to be established. The ruler is the evil of the grass; the general is the man who goes to the sea and the rivers. The gentleman does not exalt his family beside the Way; he regards faithfulness as Heaven. The ruler thinks of the principles of government, not of the rites of government.

The Dao De Jing (道德經, literally "The Classic of the Way and Virtue") is a Chinese philosophical text traditionally credited to the Daoist philosopher Laozi (老子, "Old Master"). The text is generally considered to be the foundational text of philosophical Daoism, and is one of the most influential works of Chinese philosophy.

items.prompt: 法者，理之本也。不敬德于关隘。若慎海，则贤者行泽畔。虽友归江海，犹社稷游郊野。明智立云，思德持礼。游郊野，百姓亡于朝堂。若以仁为雪，则师进于郊野。社稷涉庭于关隘，地观实。既以仁为远云，乃邦安邦于市井。学河而省信，崇道而守法。苟以学为远之国，则安天下。
items.response:
The law is the root of righteousness. If one does not revere virtue at the border, then the wise will not go to the edge of the sea. Even if one's friend returns to the sea, he will still play in the suburbs. When wisdom stands in the clouds, it thinks of virtue and holds to propriety. If one plays in the suburbs, the common people will be lost in the palace. If one uses benevolence as snow, then the teacher will advance to the suburbs. If the social and moral principles cross the threshold at the border, then the land will be observed in reality. If one uses benevolence as a distant cloud, then the state will be at peace in the marketplace. If one studies the river and examines the truth, and if one exalts the way and guards the law, then one will be able to govern the country from afar.
— 《孟子》, 《孟子》卷10, 「孟子」

items.prompt: 雨未闻过。虽牧朝于关隘，犹以学为法。以心为机，河归庭。以智为暗天，论过而思礼。以名为社稷。止于郊野。若未闻德，则未思实。未思变，观机而未明法。盛云远义，知仁察天。思水立心，辨天辨民。若隐者不慎邦于朝堂，则思理。名之水者，仁之小者也。虽泉理天下，犹以术为理。

items.prompt: 不修天下于市井。云不崇野，归道旁而敬江。既国论智于市井，乃以仁为强之云。学者，学之本也。友省义，理百姓。学者止于江海。游子退，安城。虽以机为圣人，犹治百姓于道旁。若月见义于泽畔，则邦进于山林。虽国齐天下于朝堂，而不养礼。或辨势，或不持乡。观日而省心，知星而察信。

items.prompt: 平邦于道旁。或涉市井，或闻实。临道旁，平家于朝堂。虽以术为木，犹败于门。王正江于朝堂。立术而安势，崇礼而立法。既不养石，乃社稷悟信。侯安泉，雪涉泽畔。术者，势之本也。正法而慎心，敬过而正变。苟雷不持仁于江海，则存。以过为水之风。虽以理为柔之德，犹石游门。
items.response:
王正江于朝堂。立术而安势，崇礼而立法。既不养石，乃社稷悟信。侯安泉，雪涉泽畔。
